channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: McClain Delaney Wins Democratic House Primary in Maryland Against Trone
item.description: Representative April McClain Delaney defeated David Trone, who lent himself $25 million in his unsuccessful bid to oust the woman who had succeeded him in the House.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner
item.media:credit: Leigh Vogel/Getty Images for MISTR, Free Online PrEP
item.media:description: Representative April McClain Delaney, a lawyer and former official in the Biden administration, will be favored in the general election in her Democratic-leaning district.

item.title: Moderate Democrat Wins Primary in Utah for Newly Blue Salt Lake City Seat
item.description: Ben McAdams, a former representative who had to distance himself from more conservative positions he once held, will be favored to win a blue seat created by court-ordered redistricting.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:description: Former representative Ben McAdams is running to represent Utah’s new Democratic-leaning congressional district.

item.title: Fired Navy Admiral Wins Democratic Runoff in South Carolina’s 1st District
item.description: Democrats expect Nancy Lacore to run a competitive general election despite the district’s Republican leaning.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti

item.title: Local Republican Wins Primary for Nancy Mace’s House Seat in South Carolina
item.description: Jenny Costa Honeycutt, a Charleston County councilwoman and lawyer, topped a Republican rival in a runoff primary election for a seat that leans to the right but could be competitive in November.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti

item.title: Trump’s Pick for Top I.R.S. Lawyer Works at Firm That Represents Him
item.description: James R. Gadwood, the president’s nominee for chief counsel at the Internal Revenue Service, works at Miller & Chevalier, which has represented Mr. Trump in tax matters.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Internal Revenue Service building in Washington. James R. Gadwood is the second person President Trump has nominated to the agency’s top legal job in his second term.

item.title: Ex-Hoyer Aide Buoyed by Super PACs Wins Democratic House Primary in Maryland
item.description: Adrian Boafo, a state delegate, won a primary to succeed the retiring Representative Steny Hoyer with help from more than $11 million in spending from pro-Israel and cryptocurrency interests.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner
item.media:description: Adrian Boafo’s victory in the Democratic primary means he is very likely to win a seat in Congress in November, given how overwhelmingly the seat leans to the left.

item.title: A Giraffe Named Gracie Is Missing in Texas
item.description: The giraffe wandered off a private ranch more than a week ago. Her owner is offering a $5,000 reward for information leading to her return.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri
item.media:description: The Cedar Hollow Ranch in Leakey, Texas, is missing a giraffe named Gracie.

item.title: Polling in New York’s 12th District Points to a Competitive Race
item.description: Micah Lasher and Alex Bores have consistently led in recent polls as Jack Schlossberg’s support has dipped.
item.dc:creator: Caroline Soler
item.media:description: Micah Lasher, left, and Alex Bores at a forum in Manhattan in March.

item.title: Federal Judge Bars ICE From Making Arrests in Immigration Courts
item.description: The ruling in a class-action lawsuit filed in California applies to immigration courts nationwide.
item.dc:creator: Jazmine Ulloa and Luis Ferré-Sadurní
item.media:credit: Victor J. Blue for The New York Times
item.media:description: Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents detained an immigrant from Guatemala outside a court hearing last July.

item.title: Alan Wilson Wins Republican Primary for South Carolina Governor
item.description: The runoff was one of the few races this year featuring both candidates endorsed by President Trump. His picks have had mixed results in governor’s contests.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina
item.media:description: Attorney General Alan Wilson of South Carolina in Columbia, S.C., last year.

item.title: Congress Clears Housing Bill, Cementing a Rare Bipartisan Feat
item.description: A lopsided House vote cleared the measure for President Trump’s signature after a lengthy back and forth and several nearly fatal blows to the legislation.
item.dc:creator: Ronda Kaysen
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: Factory-built apartment construction in Columbus, Ohio, earlier this year. The country is short several million new housing units, according to some estimates.

item.title: AIPAC, Crypto Cash and Rich Candidates Dominate Maryland House Races
item.description: In two competitive Democratic primaries, well-funded interest groups as well as wealthy candidates themselves have poured tens of millions of dollars into advertising.
item.dc:creator: Matt Zdun and Theodore Schleifer
item.media:credit: Gail Burton/Associated Press; Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Adrian Boafo, left, and David Trone are at the center of two expensive Democratic primaries for the House in Maryland.

item.title: Areas to Watch in a Complicated South Carolina Primary Runoff
item.description: President Trump has said he supports both Pamela Evette and Alan Wilson in the Republican primary runoff for governor.
item.dc:creator: Evan Reynolds
item.media:credit: Travis Dove for The New York Times
item.media:description: In South Carolina, Lt. Gov. Pamela Evette and Alan Wilson, the state’s attorney general, are in a runoff for the Republican nomination for governor. President Trump is supporting both candidates.

item.title: When to Expect Results in Maryland, New York, South Carolina and Utah
item.description: Voters have been casting ballots in several primaries and runoff elections. Unofficial results will probably be known late Tuesday or early Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Taylor Robinson
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voting at Public School 56 in Brooklyn on Tuesday morning. Besides New York, voters participated in primaries in Maryland and Utah, along with a runoff election in South Carolina.

item.title: U.S. Eases Travel Restrictions on Iran’s World Cup Team
item.description: The Iranians will be allowed to enter the United States two days, instead of one, before a pivotal game in Seattle on Friday, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.media:description: The Iranian national team practicing in Carson, Calif., on Saturday. U.S. officials are letting the team enter the country two days before its match against Egypt in Seattle on Friday.

item.title: Trump Is Making Big Claims About the Iran Talks. Iran Keeps Contradicting Him.
item.description: President Trump appears to be describing his preferences as fully negotiated deals, in hopes of locking the Iranians in. The question is whether a succession of such disputes will sink the whole venture.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger and Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: In the negotiation between the United States and Iran, leaks are replaced by official, if fragmentary, announcements — usually from the American side.

item.title: N.S.A. Lost Access to Powerful A.I. Model Amid Anthropic Dispute
item.description: A recent episode underscored the Trump administration’s increasing reliance on advanced A.I. systems for cybersecurity even as it battles a leading U.S. developer.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz and Julian E. Barnes
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has labeled Anthropic, the maker of Mythos 5, a supply chain risk.

item.title: Trump Says Vandals Sabotaged the Reflecting Pool. Internal Documents Raise Doubts.
item.description: The documents do not indicate that the peeling blue coating and algae blooms were caused intentionally.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and David A. Fahrenthold
item.media:description: The reflecting pool, seen from the Washington Monument, with workers attempting to contain the algae on Sunday.

item.title: Senate Votes to Direct End to Iran War, Rebuking Trump on War Powers
item.description: A few Republicans joined Democrats in favor of a measure instructing President Trump to halt military operations against Iran or seek congressional authorization.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: The Capitol seen on Tuesday. The vote came amid tensions over the war among the G.O.P., which faces a punishing political environment ahead of midterm elections.

item.title: Tucker Carlson and Marjorie Taylor Greene Say They Are Done With the G.O.P.
item.description: Their split with the Republican Party represents an expansion of a feud with President Trump that could further complicate the party’s midterm prospects.
item.dc:creator: Emily Davies
item.media:credit: Rebecca Noble for The New York Times; Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Tucker Carlson and Marjorie Taylor Greene.

item.title: Letter From Conservatives Prompted Inquiry of Civil Rights Group, Lawyers Say
item.description: Several right-wing groups sent a letter in September to Stephen Miller, a top aide to President Trump, urging greater scrutiny of the Southern Poverty Law Center.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Carolyn Van Houten for The New York Times
item.media:description: Stephen Miller, a top White House aide, has often exercised significant influence over the Justice Department.

item.title: A Look Inside the Welcome Bags Planned for White South African Refugees
item.description: The United States is putting together bags with a children’s book on so-called reverse racism, and with a document that defends the country’s founding on the basis of slavery.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz and Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.media:description: Afrikaner refugees from South Africa arriving at Dulles International Airport in Virginia last May.

item.title: How Trump Turned America’s Refugee Program Into a Pathway for White People
item.description: President Trump has created an exception to his refugee ban for white South Africans, reshaping a program intended for people fleeing persecution and disaster.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs and Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:credit: Terry Ratzlaff for The New York Times
item.media:description: A white South African, Charl Kleinhaus secured refugee status and moved with his family to the United States in a matter of months.

item.title: Protesters Accused of Antifa Ties Sentenced to Up to 100 Years in ICE Attack
item.description: The penalties, issued in an attack where a police officer was shot, dwarfed those given to Jan. 6 rioters and appeared to signal that at least some courts will deal aggressively with ICE protesters.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer and Krista Torralva
item.media:description: The attack took place last year outside the Prairieland Detention Center in Alvarado, Texas.

item.title: Vendors Told to Start Dismantling Florida’s ‘Alligator Alcatraz’ Detention Center
item.description: The directive came days after the federal government said all detainees had been transferred out of the state-run immigration detention center in the Florida Everglades.
item.dc:creator: David Ovalle and Patricia Mazzei
item.media:description: The detention facility, about halfway between Miami and Naples, has cost state officials more than $1 million per day to operate.

item.title: Polling Is Limited in New York’s Democratic House Primaries
item.description: Few independent surveys were published in Democratic primaries despite prominent candidates and some ideological tests.
item.dc:creator: Caroline Soler
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: New Yorkers cast ballots at P.S. 56 in Clinton Hill, Brooklyn, on Tuesday.

item.title: Supreme Court Sides With Exxon in Lawsuit Over Assets Seized by Cuba
item.description: The Trump administration backed Exxon Mobil’s effort to be compensated for oil and gas assets confiscated by the Cuban government in 1960.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:description: Exxon sued three companies owned by the Cuban government that it said have been exploiting its stolen refineries and service stations without compensation.

item.title: Appeals Court Allows Trump to Resume Expedited Deportations Nationwide
item.description: In a 2-to-1 vote, a federal appeals court panel ruled that the president can expand the procedure, previously used primarily near the border, to arrests nationally.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Justin Hamel for The New York Times
item.media:description: People being escorted onto a deportation flight in El Paso, Texas, in 2023.

item.title: She’s in Line to Be Mayor of D.C. How Will She Handle Trump’s Threat?
item.description: “We’re going to find ways to resist,” a defiant Janeese Lewis George, the democratic socialist who won the Democratic primary last week in Washington, said in an interview.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson
item.category: US Federal Takeover of Metropolitan Police Dept (DC)
item.media:description: “It’s going to be different,” Janeese Lewis George, the democratic socialist who won the Democratic primary for Washington mayor, said in an interview.

item.title: Supreme Court Bars Rastafarian Man From Suing Prison Guards for Shaving His Dreadlocks
item.description: Damon Landor, a Rastafarian, tried to sue Louisiana prison officials for violating his religious rights.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Damon Landor sought to sue state prison officials in Louisiana for forcibly shaving his head.

item.title: Justice Dept. Unveils Medicare Fraud Charges as Part of Larger Health Care Prosecutions
item.description: The department is taking aim at pricey “skin substitutes,” an overused wound care treatment that cost the government nearly $15 billion in 2025.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff
item.media:description: Mehmet Oz, center, the head of the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, with Todd Blanche, right, the acting attorney general, and Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. during a briefing at the Justice Department on Tuesday.

item.title: Supreme Court Rejects Lawsuit Claiming Cisco Systems Helped China Target Falun Gong
item.description: The court’s decision could have broader implications for whether companies can be held liable for aiding in international human rights abuses.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Falun Gong followers during a silent protest outside the Communist Party’s headquarters in Beijing in 1999.

item.title: Justice Dept. Issued, Then Withdrew, Grand Jury Subpoenas of Journalists
item.description: The administration backed down after the news organizations, The Wall Street Journal and The Washington Post, fought them in sealed filings.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:description: The Justice Department had sought information related to reporting on top officials warning President Trump of the risks of a major military campaign against Iran and on U.S. military action in Venezuela.

item.title: Major Supreme Court Decisions Testing Trump’s Policies Remain
item.description: Over the next two weeks, the justices will release more than a dozen final opinions, including high-profile decisions on birthright citizenship, the Federal Reserve and transgender athletes.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:description: The justices will resolve a series of high-profile cases testing the Trump administration’s policies to expand presidential power and reshape the federal bureaucracy.

item.title: The A.I. Boom Boosted Teacher Pay in a Rural County. Can It Last?
item.description: The large bonuses tied to data center construction in Richland Parish, La., are unprecedented, but critics say it is not a panacea.
item.dc:creator: Jacey Fortin
item.media:credit: Greg Hilburn/USA Today Network, via Imagn Images
item.media:description: Construction at Meta’s data center last year in Richland Parish, La.
